data.title: Vicor: The Narrative Has Changed (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I haven't covered Vicor Corporation (NASDAQ:VICR) for an extended period. In early May, I initiated the stock with a sell rating as the technicals were bearish, margin contraction was worrying, and the valuation was unattractive. For their second quarter in July, they reported "blowout" results, and as of this writing, the stock has gained over 100% since the publishing of my previous article, as Vicor is surging after Q3 earnings. Today, we'll examine their most recent results to see if the narrative for the company has changed.

In the below analysis, it is shown that Vicor is turning around nicely. Top-line growth is accelerating, their backlog is increasing, and profitability is now on a good trajectory. While Vicor's P/E ratios are significantly above the sector median, they are well below their own 5-year average. With the business outlook stronger than it has been in recent memory, I would say there may be room for multiple expansions. Lastly, the technicals are also bullish currently, and so I have decided to double upgrade Vicor to a buy rating.

Demand Continues To Improve

Firstly, YCharts hasn't updated their charts yet since earnings were reported just yesterday (Oct 21st), but nonetheless, I believe they provide a good reference point for where their business is at. For their Q3, Vicor generated total revenues of $110.4 million, representing an 18.5% increase YoY. That is a mix of product revenues and licensing income.

CEO Patrizio Vinciarelli stated that "licensing revenue reached a record rate in Q3 following the litigation settlement for prior infringement recorded in Q2." Vinciarelli also expects "Vicor's IP licensing practice to grow substantially" due to the use of their high-density power systems IP for high-performance computing. Therefore, it should be clear by now that licensing is becoming an increasingly important part of their business.

As you can see above, the top-line growth rate for the previous quarter was just 11.87% (when excluding patent litigation settlement), and also note that Q3's increase is basically the largest in the past three years. As of now, it seems that demand is on a great trajectory, and it looks particularly encouraging considering that 2024 was really a year to forget. Lastly, their top line was also able to beat expectations by a quite nice margin of $15.02 million. That isn't a small beat for a company of their size and shows that business activity is strong overall.

Other than their top-line results, another metric that shows demand is strong is their backlog. At the end of their Q3, they had a total backlog of $152.8 million, representing a 1.5% increase YoY. Back in Q2, they saw a 0.9% YoY increase, and so this acceleration in backlog expansion shows that customers are essentially starting to line up for their products. That is, of course, a very encouraging sign.

Margins & The Bottom Line

The last time I analyzed Vicor's fundamentals, it all fell apart starting in their margins, so let's see if they have been able to stabilize or expand their margins. In terms of their gross margin, they reported 57.5% for Q3, which is a major expansion from the prior year period's 49.1%. This was a bit smaller than the expansion seen in Q2, but nonetheless, the days of margin contraction are now clearly over. This signals strong cost control and/or pricing power, and given the improving demand, it isn't overly surprising to see gross margin expand. Still, many companies are struggling with costs due to tariffs, and so Vicor's gross margin expansion is very nice to see in this tough macro environment.

In terms of their corporate efficiency, the company saw their operating expenses increase from $40.4 million to $42.6 million YoY. That's only a 5.4% increase, and when considering that top-line growth was 18.5%, it should be clear that profitability is on the right path now. When you look at their breakdown of operating expenses, it is even more encouraging. SG&A expenses actually dropped YoY, while R&D spending increased by 17.1%. Therefore, Vicor is spending in the right places and is essentially investing for future growth.

With revenue growth being at three-year highs and margins expanding significantly, it should be no surprise that the bottom line for Vicor saw a massive jump YoY. They reported a diluted EPS of $0.63, and that represents a whopping 142.3% increase YoY. They absolutely destroyed expectations as they beat by $0.52. With revenues beating by a much smaller margin in percentage terms, this shows that analysts were likely very pleasantly surprised with how strong Vicor's profitability has been. As a whole, I would say the company is seeing promising signs throughout their earnings report thus far in my analysis. As you can see above, consensus estimates show that there may be some rockiness in bottom-line growth over the next few quarters. I would say investors should take these expectations with a grain of salt, as the company itself did not provide any quarterly guidance due to the uncertain timing of additional license deals.

Cash Flows Are Robust

Cash is king, and Vicor's cash flows also don't disappoint. Their operating cash flow was reported to be $38.5 million, an increase of 70.4% YoY. While that isn't as impressive as Q2's 317.9% jump, it does show that the cash generation ability of the business is strong right now. Furthermore, the company also decreased their CAPEX from $8.4 million to $4.0 million YoY, and so after subtracting these figures from the operating cash flow, the free cash flow growth rate is calculated to be 143.0%. That is a major surge in FCF that investors should be happy about. In fact, the company bought back $15.6 million in stock as a return of capital to shareholders. Vicor doesn't currently pay a dividend, but the share repurchases are really a sign of confidence in future cash flows and perhaps an indication that they believe the stock is still undervalued.

Valuation May Not Be Expensive

So far, so good for Vicor, but there may be some concerns regarding the stock's valuation. As you can see above, Seeking Alpha rates their overall valuation as a D+, and all of their P/E ratios are significantly higher than the Industrials sector's median. That said, currently the company does seem to be firing on all cylinders. Top-line growth is accelerating, backlog is increasing, margin is expanding, and the bottom line obliterated expectations. Therefore, some premium may not be surprising.

Despite some concerns regarding their valuation relative to the Industrials sector, as you can see in the far-right column of the above table, Vicor's P/E ratios are actually far below their five-year averages, and so relative to historical levels, the stock's valuation may be cheap. Given that the trajectory of their business hasn't looked this good in quite a while, I would say that this valuation discount relative to the five-year average suggests that there is likely to be multiple expansions moving forward. That should bode well for investors.

Technical Analysis

Vicor stock has gapped up as a result of strong Q3 earnings, and the technicals are looking quite favorable currently. For the long term, the 50-day SMA and 200-day SMA had a golden cross in late September as a bullish signal. On the current trading day, both the MACD and RSI have hit six-month highs as a confirmation signal for the major rally. Therefore, a bearish reversal likely isn't in the cards for the near future.

For the near term, things are obviously bullish. The 9-day EMA has soared far above the 21-day EMA, and the MACD and RSI are both in very strong positions, as stated just a moment ago. Do note that the stock has soared far above the Bollinger Bands upper band, and so there is potential for an overbought pullback or at least some consolidation before the stock rallies again. Overall, the technical setup is a positive one, as there is likely room to run over the longer term.

Conclusion

In my previous article, I found major issues in their margins, and the technicals were bearish. For Q3, it is clear that they fixed their profitability issues, and their other fundamentals are also on a great trajectory. As discussed above, the technicals are also looking bullish currently. The stock's valuation may be a bit elevated relative to the industrials sector, but note that their licensing business is now directly involved with high-performance computing, and so that comparison may not be overly meaningful. Instead, their P/E ratios are far below the historic averages, suggesting the valuation is still cheap while the turnaround is gaining steam. With the outlook for the stock looking bright, I have decided to double upgrade to a buy.

data.title: Energy Transfer: Discount Isn't What You Think
data.description:
Investment Thesis

What puzzles me about Energy Transfer (NYSE:ET) is why it trades at a lower valuation than its peers. The sector isn't a growth powerhouse, but if that's the benchmark, ET is winning, according to Seeking Alpha's Growth Quant Score.

Company Quant Growth Score
Targa Resources Corp. (TRGP) A
Plains All American Pipeline (PAA) A+
Energy Transfer LP B-
Kinder Morgan, Inc. (KMI) C
Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) C-
MPLX LP (MPLX) C+
ONEOK, Inc. (OKE) C+
The Williams Companies (WMB) C+
Pembina Pipeline Corp. (PBA) D
Cheniere Energy Partners (CQP) D-
TC Energy Corporation (TRP) D-
Enbridge Inc. (ENB) D+
Western Midstream Partners (WES) D+

One reason ET shares are down this year is management's comments on the outlook. Last August, the company said that it expected adjusted EBITDA to fall at the lower bound of its previous guidance of $16.1 to $16.5 billion or slightly lower.

While this explains the price drop, it doesn't explain why ET has persistently traded below peers' multiples, even in periods when it raised or maintained guidance.

I looked at segment results. The pipelines look fine in terms of quality and location. The discount seems to be tied to governance issues, including the Williams Companies' saga (2016-2023), the 2020 dividend cut, and the FERC rate refund case, all of which have left a bitter taste in investors' mouths, in my view. The dual role of ET's founder, Kelcy Warren, as an executive and the Chairman of the board, with a significant influence on both functions, creates governance risk.

I'm bullish on ET's 8% yield, and the 7.4% yield on its Series I preferreds (NYSE:ET.PR.I). A high single-digit capital gain isn't off the table either. After all, this is one of the largest, most diversified midstream operators in the US, if not the world, yet it carries one of the lowest valuation multiples compared to other large-cap midstream operators.

Lower Guidance Explain The Drop, Not Discount

One reason ET lowered its guidance is the disappointing performance of the crude oil segment, which saw EBITDA down 10.6% this year. The company cited weaker crude oil volumes from the Bakken region, which were impacted by extreme weather conditions earlier this year, in addition to fewer optimization (trading and arbitrage) opportunities.

Crude oil prices are also down, and this dampens the sector's outlook.

The crude oil segment is important to ET, with an EBITDA contribution in the 20% range (18.5% in Q2 '25). It also houses ET's flagship asset, the Dakota Access Pipeline, which connects the Bakken region in North Dakota to export terminals on the Gulf Coast through its sister pipeline, ETCO.

ET's ~20% exposure to the now-struggling crude oil midstream market doesn't explain its valuation discount.

Plains All American has a higher EBITDA crude oil concentration and Bakken exposure, yet it trades at higher price multiples than ET.

If ET's crude segment is what's dragging down its valuation multiples, then one would expect PAA and OKE to trade at even lower valuations, given their larger exposure to crude oil.

Finally, most of ET's crude oil assets are in regions that have experienced an increase in oil production, particularly the Eagle Ford and Permian basins in Texas. In fact, oil running through ET's pipelines increased in H1 '25 by 9%.

Intrastate gas: Why ET Diverged From Peers?

Intrastate Transport and Storage refers to intrastate natural gas pipelines, mostly in Texas and Oklahoma.

The intrastate segment was down 18% in H1 '25, with management again citing fewer trading opportunities due to the lack of volatility in natural gas prices.

However, this time, ET's performance and management's explanation contradict what peers are reporting. For example, in the second quarter of 2025, KMI explicitly highlighted the Texas Intrastate Network as a contributor to growth.

The performance of ET's segment was an anomaly, not the standard.

The Natural Gas Pipelines business segment’s improved financial performance in the second quarter of 2025 relative to the second quarter of 2024 was due primarily to continued higher contributions from both our Texas Intrastate system - Tom Martin, KMI President, Q2 '25 Press Release.

Enterprise Products Partners ) also highlighted the strength of its Texas Intrastate system:

Gross operating margin from the Texas Intrastate System increased $21 million primarily due to higher transportation-related revenues. EPD Q2 '25 SEC Filing (p. 4)

This suggests that ET's intrastate segment is more sensitive to price differentials and optimization opportunities compared to peers.

Midstream: Stable Contracts With Price Exposure

ET's midstream segment encompasses sales from the gathering and processing of "raw" natural gas through a network of small, low-pressure pipelines.

The segment provides a stable income stream due to the long-term contracts covering dedicated acreage owned by oil & gas producers. However, these exclusivity covenants are often attached to a Percentage of Proceeds "POP” arrangement, exposing ET to natural gas and NGL prices. This is a standard arrangement within the industry, and fortunately, it has been a tailwind this year.

Historically, the midstream segment contributed roughly 20% of EBITDA (21.2% in H1 '25).

Recently, natural gas prices have been increasing. Production volumes across key operating regions have been increasing as well. For these reasons, the segment's EBITDA grew 22% in H1 '25, reaching ~$1.7 billion.

ET's biggest gas-gathering networks are in Texas and Oklahoma. In Texas, the latest data from the EIA show continued production momentum, with volumes up 5% in the seven months ended July 2025 compared to the same period of 2024. We also see strong growth in Oklahoma, where ET maintains its Mid-Continent gathering network, with production volumes up 2.6%.

Things aren't as rosy in Wyoming, where ET runs the Powder River Basin network, with production volumes down 2.4%. In Louisiana, where ET runs its Ark-la-Tex gas-gathering network, volumes are flat to slightly down (-0.6%) this year, according to the latest figures. Similarly, volumes in North Dakota were flat to slightly up (0.6%).

Still, despite some exposure to troubled regions, the size of this exposure is relatively modest. It is unlikely that such a small exposure explains hundreds of millions, or even billions of dollars, in ET's valuation discount to peers.

Midstream segment pipeline Net Gas Processing Capacity (MMcf/d)
South Texas 2,530
Ark-La-Tex 922
North Central Texas 700
Permian Basin 4,945
Midcontinent 2,865
Williston Basin 400
Powder River Basin 345
Eastern 200

NGL & Refined Petroleum: Higher Volumes, Softer Gains

The NGL segment saw EBITDA down slightly (-2.3%) in the six months ended June 2025, again, due to lower trading and arbitrage, offset by higher NGL volumes. This time, the trend is consistent with peers operating in the same regions.

NGL represented about 25% of consolidated EBITDA in H1 '25.

Return on assets (defined as adjusted EBITDA/average segment assets) stood at 7.5% in the six months ended June 2025, similar to the same period of last year. When annualized, this 15% rate is also comparable to the past two years.

What Sunoco Adds, and What ET Keeps

ET consolidates Sunoco LP (SUN) in its financial report because it controls the company via 100% ownership of the general partner units, and 20.9% of the common units (28.5 million units owned by ET out of 136.5 million units outstanding).

Sunoco's EBITDA increased by 62% to $912 million in H1 '25. However, what ET takes home is much less, given that the EBITDA increase was a result of the NuStar acquisition, which was tied to a significant dilution in common units.

The GP interest has an incremental claim over distributions. ET is entitled to 50% of the per common unit distributions above 0.65625/unit. In Q2 '25, SUN distributed 0.9088/unit, up 3% YoY.

For all intents and purposes, SUN's quarterly per unit distribution growth is the main metric to assess the company as a subsidiary of ET, rather than the reported adjusted EBITDA.

Interstate Pipelines: Stronger Throughput

The Interstate segment was up $107 million, or 12.2%, in H1 '25. Management attributes this to increased utilization and, to a lesser extent, the lapping of the FERC rate case refunds last quarter, which contributed $35 million to the segment's growth this year. Gas interstate pipelines are FERC-regulated, and ET was ordered to refund its customers. This situation is rare and somewhat raises concerns over governance and ET's relationship with its customers.

For the three months ended June 30, 2025 compared to the same period last year, Segment Adjusted EBITDA related to our interstate transportation and storage segment increased due to the net impact of the following: an increase of $70 million in segment margin primarily due to a $35 million negative impact in the prior period related to the conclusion of a rate case on our Panhandle system. Q2 '25 SEC Filing

Governance Scars

ET's dividend cuts in 2020 left a bad taste in investors' mouths. I think the entire episode just touched on past traumas by the management team. Personally, I never liked how management handled the Williams acquisition bid, and the preferred stock saga, which, in December 2021, the Delaware Court criticized in strong terms, as quoted below. In 2023, the Delaware Supreme Court reaffirmed the judge's ruling and ordered ET to pay WMB $410 million, plus lawyers' fees.

[...] For these reasons, I found in the Unitholder action that the Preferred Offering “was a hedge meant to protect [ETE] insiders from the anticipated bad effects of the coming merger”—an “opportunity to eliminate downside risk” that ETE “insiders seized” “for themselves and their cronies”. Indeed, by transforming the ETE common units held by insiders into preferred units, ETE gained the ability to cut distributions to zero on ETE common units, along with its matching obligation regarding ETC dividends, while shielding its own insiders from the downside. - Vice Chancellor Sam Glasscock III, Delaware Court Memorandum Opinion.

This is a reminder that, in capital markets, the benefits of dividends extend beyond monetary benefits - they reduce the agency-principal dilemma.

Income First, with Ample Coverage

For those who don't buy into management's modest growth pitch, I think ET's Preferred shares offer an attractive alternative, and have a yield of 7.4%.

The preferreds are safe, in my view. In H1 '25, ET generated $7.9 billion in EBITDA, while cash interest stood at $1.6 billion and maintenance CapEx was $507 million, leaving the preferred distributions, which stood at a mere $134 million, with ample coverage.

Distributable cash flows stood at $4.3 billion, providing solid coverage for the $2.2 billion common unit distribution in H1 '25.

Q3 '25 Preview

ET is set to release its Q3 '25 earnings on November 5, 2025. Consensus estimates point to $21.8 billion in sales, and an EPS of 0.33/share.

My EBITDA estimate puts Q3 '25 at around $4.1 billion. That's based on management's commentary on the FY '25 EBITDA outlook falling at, or slightly below the previous $16.1-$16.5 billion guidance.

In H1 '25, adjusted EBITDA was $7.97 billion (Q1: $4.1 billion, Q2: $3.87 billion). Based on a $16 billion EBITDA FY forecast, H2 '25 EBITDA should be around $8 billion. Operations are mostly stable throughout the year, so, an equal weight between Q3 and Q4 suggests EBITDA around $4 billion.

Oil prices have been declining, and this might impact volumes. However, this will probably be offset by new natural gas processing facilities, including Lenorah and Badger, which came online in Q2, with Q3 capturing full operations. Latest data from EIA also point to upward-trending NGL exports, also providing a tailwind for ET. On the balance of these dynamics, we see some softness, but not a major cliff.

Risks

The Buy rating is based on the stability and critical nature of ET's operations, as well as its mostly fee-based earnings. Capital gains are on the table. ET's discount isn't about an issue with its assets, but instead, governance issues that created past scars. As ET continues to execute and establish its position as a stable dividend payer, the valuation discount could potentially recover.

The main risk is an event that brings past trauma to investors' minds, such as a complex deal that puts the dividend at risk. ET's leadership mishandled the WMB acquisition, and it will take time for the shareholder base to recover from this.

ET is also awaiting environmental review of its Dakota Access Line, its flagship pipeline, after the Supreme Court refused to hear ET's appeal. Politically, the winds are in ET's favor, but ET isn't out of the woods yet. The pipeline is currently allowed to continue operations, but ET may need to invest in environmental protection initiatives.

Final Thoughts

This year hasn't been kind to many midstream companies, after hedge funds trimmed sector exposure as crude oil declined. The good news is that there is no fundamental issue in the midstream market. ET, for example, is reporting record volumes across its network. Energy exports are near all-time highs, and rig count and crude oil production remain stable after a short period of decline, despite the energy price volatility. My point is, there really might be an opportunity in ET's 8% dividend.

There's always going to be an operator building a new pipeline, or twinning an old one, but I don't feel these initiatives are disruptive. The industry leaders are financially prudent when it comes to capacity building and contract negotiations. I don't think many investors appreciate that, except those who have seen how bad things can be for capital-intensive industries.

data.title: Colgate-Palmolive: Shine Still Missing After The Slide - Remains A Sell
data.description:
Intro

On 14 July 2025, my first article about Colgate-Palmolive (NYSE:CL) was published. It was the first "sell"-rated article since November 2021. So a very contrarian viewpoint. As it turned out, my sell rating, was correct.

CL stock, as expected, has shown weakness, falling from $88.85 by ten bucks to $78.75 as of writing. That's a drop of 11.3% in only three months.

While on the surface nothing dramatic happened, I want to reiterate that perceived high-quality or defensive stocks should not be bought blindly "because nothing can go wrong". Many things can go wrong. If not on the business side, a too expensive stock can dilute your stock portfolio's returns.

CL stock is such an example. While the common narrative is that this is a solid hold or even buy, I am keeping my "sell" rating. This slow-grower does not deserve such a high multiple above 20x, as I will show.

Empty Tube

Since my article came online, CL published their Q2/H1 2025 results. No big surprises in any direction.

Growth continues to be anemic with low single digits. Gross margins are slightly under pressure through tariffs, although the headwinds turned out to be lower than initially expected. Colgate continues to keep a strong and steady market share in toothpaste and manual toothbrushes. But there's no dynamic in this saturated market.

The guidance adjustment was to the downside. Management got a bit more cautious, guiding to the lower end of the previously communicated financial targets. All in all, no drama, but also no push forward.

To add a few details, CL's Q2 2025 earnings call revealed a consumer company navigating tough waters. From a positive view, net sales, organic sales, and EPS grew despite raw material cost spikes, negative foreign exchange, and a private label drag. Pricing strength helped counter headwinds. But that's treading water, not something to push the stock forward.

A bit more interesting were the results from Hill’s Pet Nutrition, posting 5% organic growth in an otherwise flat category. Therapeutic products and innovation in wet food and cat segments led the charge. The pet segment has a revenue share of ~23% and generates 24% of operating earnings. So, it is not insignificant. However, as it is not the core business or competency of CL, it might make sense to sell it. This could maybe reignite some stock fireworks, but we'll need to see. These are just my thoughts.

A month later at the Barclays 18th Annual Global Consumer Staples Conference 2025, management did not say much new. CL’s 2025 strategy (initially from 2019) delivered consistent 3-5% organic growth, driven by innovation, AI-enhanced revenue growth management, and increased advertising. A new $200-$300 million productivity and cost savings initiative shall fund 2030 goals focused on omni-channel demand generation and premiumization. Despite a challenging consumer environment, CL is optimistic about sustaining top- and bottom-line growth.

But let's be honest, growth should not be overestimated. Low- to at best mid-single digit rates are to look out for. Over the last five years, sales growth went through a rollercoaster, first falling, then pushed through inflationary price adjustments, to finally start falling again. Unfortunately, CL now reached the lowest level over this period, casting some doubt about the growth targets.

With little to no sales growth, and falling operating margins, we should definitely be more on the conservative side when valuing the stock. Past performance and history should not be the guide, but reality and hard facts and figures.

Free cash flow has left the temporary trough from the high-inflation period. But it's tilting to the downside again, making growth ambitions anything but sure.

Valuation

I have done a DCF analysis to determine the fair value of CL stock. My input parameters are:

  • $3.4 billion FCF to start with (last twelve-month figure)
  • 5% growth over ten years
  • then 3%
  • 10% discount rate (my return requirement)

What we get is a fair value of $56 billion—this is an enterprise value figure (the sum of all cash flows for the entire business).

This means, the current market cap of $64 billion plus net debt of around $7 billion results in a current EV of $71 billion. This is significantly above the DCF model's fair value of $56 billion EV ($49 billion market cap or equity value).

$49 billion equity value suggests a 20% downside from the current level, which I do not see as unreasonable or unrealistic. This would be a share price in the low- to mid-$60s (assuming no big share count shrinkage). Before, I would definitely not buy.

Please keep in mind that I used 5% as my growth figure for the next ten years. This could be too aggressive, implying the fair value at lower growth rates would likely be sub-$60.

Or to do a quick back-of-the-envelope calculation in the head, $3.4 billion FCF vs. an EV of $71 billion is a multiple of 21x. I deem this too expensive for the growth rates we have in front of us. That's why I continue to rate CL stock a "sell", until we see a more attractive valuation and, especially, a lower-risk setup. The dividend yields less than 3%, not offering a compelling pro argument either.

Risks To My Thesis

If Colgate-Palmolive is successful in its transformation and manages to achieve higher growth rates than currently is the case, the downside might be much lower than I have estimated.

A sale of the pet business could lead to a re-rating by the market because CL would receive a few billion in cash and the company's structure would simplify. The market likes that.

Conclusion

CL stock remains a "sell" because the business dynamic has been falling again recently. Growth is anemic, and I even question management's growth targets. But even in an assumed 5% growth scenario, the fair value of the stock likely is 20% below the current share price.

data.title: Satellites And Communications: Stellar Stocks Flying High On AI
data.description:
Communication Services, Satcom Industry Outperformance

Communication services is the top-performing sector over the past year, powered primarily by a widening cast of networks, towers, and satellite communications (Satcom) names. While semiconductors dominate AI-related headlines, the Satcom industry has quietly risen to the top of performance as hyperscale buildouts have triggered heavy data center investment needs in power, connectivity, and scalability.

This has created multi-year demand for the gear and services that move, cache, and secure the data. AI is not only boosting server and power budgets, but it’s also expanding the demand for satellite operators (connectivity and timing), satcom equipment vendors (gateways and terminals), and space-data platforms (imagery and wireless signals). By supplying high-capacity support through satellite, optic fiber, and related equipment, communications companies help to convert the surging AI and non-AI workloads into reliable, scalable bandwidth.

Satcom Supporting AI and Non-AI Workload Growth

For example, the communications equipment players supply the physical layers, such as fiber, optics, and hardware, that convert AI data center capex into real, scalable bandwidth and connectivity. Meanwhile, satellite operators and satcom equipment providers extend AI networks beyond fiber’s reach, relaying huge amounts of information from remote locations, such as cell towers, remote sites, and satellites. As AI campuses proliferate across regions, often in power-rich but connectivity-scarce areas, these players manage growing bandwidth and reliability needs.

As you can see in the chart above, the Procure Space ETF (UFO) and the SPDR S&P Telecom ETF (XTL), which are proxies for satellite stocks and the broader communications equipment industry, have respectively outperformed semiconductors (SMH), as well as the broader AI segment (AIQ) and tech sector (XLK) over the past year.

Stocks in the communication services sector, particularly those related to satellites and communication equipment, have market-crushing momentum, but many still offer reasonable valuations. Whether these are momentum plays or added to a list of buys for a potential correction, investors are wise to understand the communications sector, its role in the AI infrastructure buildout, and how to benefit from it. For this reason, I’ve put together a short list of satellite stocks and other communications equipment leaders.

How I Chose the Top 5 Satellite and Comm Equipment Stocks

Since satellite and communications equipment stocks have such strong recent performance, their momentum Factor Grades are mostly outstanding. However, valuations are a mix of attractive to slightly above average. For this reason, I first screened for Strong Buys and used the growth and/or profitability scores to guide the selection criteria.

1. Viasat, Inc. (VSAT)

  • Market Capitalization: $4.92B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 10/22/2025): 3 out of 540

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 10/22/2025): 1 out of 40

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Communications Equipment

A global satellite network operator with mobility and government roots, Viasat is directly positioned as a direct beneficiary of AI and data-hungry workloads that need reach and bandwidth beyond fiber. In addition to providing military and commercial markets with high-speed satellite broadband services and secure networking systems, VSAT offers its mix of bandwidth and network management to the AI buildout, especially as they extend into remote areas.

Beyond its stellar performance, VSAT’s strengths that support its Strong Buy rating are its valuation and profitability metrics. While the forward P/E of 29.58 places it above the sector median of 25.49, its forward EV/EBITDA of 6.91, less than half that of the sector median, demonstrates how VSAT is undervalued compared to its peers. Its price/sales, and price/book measures tell the same story. Meanwhile, VSAT’s profitability is exhibited by its $1.02B cash from operations, which is more than 750% higher than the peer median of $1.34M.

As noted in my recent article, Renewed Tariff Fears Spotlight Top Performing Stocks, VSAT shares have benefited from fresh backlog and capacity catalysts, including a prime contract award by the U.S. Space Force Space Systems Command for the Protected Tactical SATCOM-Global (PTS-G) program. Looking ahead, its ViaSat 3 F2 satellite is expected to double its fleet bandwidth with a launch in late October. These factors can combine to continue VSAT’s peer-beating momentum over the past year:

VSAT vs. Communications Equipment Peers: 1-Year Performance

For similar reasoning, we stay inside the satellite segment of the communications equipment industry for another top stock.

2. Gilat Satellite Networks Ltd. (GILT)

  • Market Capitalization: $881.58M

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 10/22/2025): 19 out of 540

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 10/22/2025): 5 out of 40

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Communications Equipment

Gilat sits on the ground segment side of the satcom industry, supplying modems, gateways, baseband platforms, and managed solutions that turn space capacity into usable enterprise and carrier services. That matters in the AI world, as 5G cell sites, remote facilities, micro-data centers, and mobility hubs all need satellites’ ability to manage huge data flows. This makes GILT a picks-and-shovels AI play.

GILT’s Factor Grades are solid, so we’ll start with metrics that make it stand out from many AI-related stocks: Its valuation. While its P/E is just slightly below its sector peers, my favorite value metric, FWD PEG, is a healthy 1.10, compared to 1.80 for the sector. Barring a widespread AI stock meltdown, this suggests further room to run on price, especially considering its growth factor. GILT’s forward revenue growth is projected at 23.25%, which is about triple that of the sector median’s 7.38%. Helping to support GILT’s performance outlook is its recent announcement that its commercial division has received $42 million in orders from a leading satellite operator, primarily for its multi-orbit SkyEdge IV platform. The systems are scheduled for delivery over the next 12 months. All the above contribute to its potential for continuing the momentum it’s built over the last 12 months.

GILT vs. Communications Equipment Peers: 1-Year Performance

Based on some of the outstanding fundamentals, plus momentum, for the communications equipment industry stocks to make this list, there’s no surprise to find the following Alpha Pick made the cut.

3. CommScope Holding Company, Inc. (COMM)

  • Market Capitalization: $3.46B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 10/22/2025): 4 out of 540

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 10/22/2025): 2 out of 40

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Communications Equipment

CommScope is the connective tissue of the terrestrial networks: Fiber and copper-structured cabling, antennas, and connectivity hardware that translate capex into real capacity. This is because AI data centers don’t just need GPUs; they need cable, fiber, robust power, and paths to move huge traffic from campus to campus. The company also benefits from cable and fixed wireless providers boosting upstream capacity, AI-adjacent small businesses, and high-end consumers.

Many of the qualities I saw in COMM when I made it on my top picks for H2 2025 are still present. For example, COMM’s valuation is still attractive, as its forward P/E of 13.89 is less than half that of the sector median. While its growth metrics have trimmed off a bit, they still add up to a B+ Factor Grade. Leading the score here is COMM’s forward free cash flow per share growth rate of 26.06%, which is 70% better than the sector median of 15.26%. The FCF metric is an indicator of the company’s long-term potential and can help investors avoid “value traps.” These qualities also explain why I singled COMM out recently in my article, 5 Best AI Revolution Stocks With Room To Run.

Addressing a valid point made by a reader in that article, CommScope announced in its August earnings call that it would divest of its CCS (Cable and Connectivity Solutions) business. What this does to support COMM’s growth and forward momentum is that the transaction effectively removes the excessive leverage that was looming over CommScope. The net $10B result is that it enabled management to raise full-year CommScope adjusted EBITDA guidance to a range of $1.15 billion to $1.2 billion. It also plans to distribute excess cash as a dividend to shareholders within 60 to 90 days post CCS transaction closing.

While near-term performance isn’t expected to repeat the last few months’ gigantic gain, the forward momentum can continue as COMM is a reasonably valued growth play.

COMM vs. Communications Peers: 1-Year Performance

COMM isn’t the last of the communications equipment industry stocks with a Strong Buy rating, plus potential for more room to run.

4. Ciena Corporation (CIEN)

  • Market Capitalization: $23.94B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 10/22/2025): 25 out of 540

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 10/22/2025): 4 out of 40

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Communications Equipment

Ciena makes the ultra-fast "roads" that carry Internet traffic over long distances: Through roads, under seas, and through cities. Most importantly, the communications equipment company provides the links that connect data centers so AI supercenters in different locations can work together as one. The company also lines up well with how networks are spreading out to the “edge,” as satellite links, 5G networks, and big business networks all need reliable connections. The net result is that Ciena is a high-quality, mission-critical supplier geared to the same multi-year AI capex cycle that’s forcing networks to be faster, smarter, and more scalable.

Like a few of our other communications equipment stocks in our list, CIEN’s rich valuation (forward P/E of 68.04) will be high for value-conscious investors, but the growth and profitability stories explain how it is a Quant Strong Buy. This makes CIEN more of a high-beta play for investors accepting the higher risk/return profile, justifying an AI beneficiary long-term play. For example, CIEN’s growth Factor Grade of A is largely supported by its forward 3-5 year long-term EPS growth projection of 47.29%, which is over 200% ahead of industry peers. Its forward free cash flow per share of 99.56% is another industry peer-crushing metric at over 500% larger.

Looking ahead, CFO Marc Graff stated in its Q3 earnings call that management expects "approximately 17% year-on-year growth in fiscal 2026, similar to what we're currently projecting in fiscal '25," with an initial gross margin estimate of 43%, plus or minus 1 point. This helps support the potential for forward price momentum, which has outpaced its communications equipment peers, other than VSAT, another stock on my list.

CIEN vs. Communications Equipment Industry Peers: 1-Year Performance

To finish our list of top stocks, we feature an unlikely but well-deserved consideration for investors looking for companies that can benefit from the satellite and communications demand from AI.

5. Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN)

  • Market Capitalization: $2.37T

  • Quant Rating: Hold

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 10/22/2025): 4 out of 484

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 10/22/2025): 1 out of 25

  • Sector: Consumer Discretionary

  • Industry: Broadline Retail

While this Magnificent Seven stock’s Quant Rating slid from Strong Buy to Hold overnight given its stretched valuation, it’s an understatement to say that AMZN is more than just an online retailer. This week’s massive outage from AWS (Amazon Web Services) illustrated its broad reach beyond retail, and provides reasoning for why the stock rose in price, rather than falling, on the day of the outage. For example, beyond AWS’s AI central compute and storage, Amazon’s Project Kuiper adds a powerful dimension as a low Earth orbit satellite broadband network. Its mission is to deliver fast, reliable internet to customers and communities around the world. To accomplish this, Amazon aims to launch commercial services by 2026 with a planned 3,236 satellite constellation, which will serve as an AWS integration. It's also important to note from this week’s outage - this could be an enhancement.

Another Quant Strong Buy with a rich valuation, AMZN earns its stellar rating through its promising growth factor. AMZN’s forward revenue growth measure of 10.77% is more than triple the industry median of 3.02%. Its forward operating cash flow is projected to grow at a pace of 26.83%, crushing the peer median of 3.87%. Commercial momentum at Amazon’s core business (retail, ads, cloud) not only helps improve its price momentum but also provides a foundation for the financial and ecosystem needed to grow and monetize Kuiper terminals. Amazon’s multi-front model gives investors diversified exposure to both AI infrastructure and consumption. While AMZN’s one-year performance isn’t as strong as some industry peers, and certainly not at the extreme levels of some AI-related stocks, it’s not completely dependent on AI for growth. This helps to make AMZN a compelling long-term idea and earn its Quant Strong Buy status.

AMZN vs. Broadline Retail Industry Peers: 1-Year Performance

AMZN is a good place to close out our top five satellite and communications equipment industry stocks as the retail giant expands into the space of our other four featured companies. They all are stars in their respective industries, but also have a place in the future of artificial intelligence.

Conclusion: AI and the Top Satellite and Communications Equipment Stocks

AI isn’t only about buying more servers and power; it also boosts demand for satellites (VSAT and GILT) and the equipment that connects them to networks (COMM and CIEN). Mega-cap companies (AMZN) that have the financial strength to capitalize on these AI picks and shovels segments stand to benefit in the long run. Satellite leaders will extend reach and resilience where fiber can’t go, while ground equipment will turn raw space capacity into usable, secure connections. Together, these “plumbing and powerlines of the Internet” can convert AI investment into real world portfolio performance.

If you’re willing to focus on stocks with solid earnings and growth potential, in times when fear is moving markets, owning stocks with strong investment characteristics and Quant Strong Buy recommendations could be fruitful. You can filter them using Seeking Alpha's Ratings Screener tool to help find stocks that achieve diversification into the desired sectors you like. Alternatively, if you want to leverage Seeking Alpha's powerful quantitative stock-picking system to deliver two data-driven stock picks each month, Alpha Picks offers a more efficient approach to smarter investing.

data.title: Inflection Points: Tariffs In The Wind
data.description:
Six months after the White House unveiled a tariff schedule for global trading partners, this seems like a good time to check in on the state of play. The initial announcement on April 2 sent equity markets sharply lower, but a week later, on April 9, markets calmed as the White House put many tariffs on hold to allow for negotiations with major trading partners.1 Despite lingering concerns that tariffs would slow international trade and drag the global economy since then, both the S&P 500 and the MSCI World indexes have advanced by more than 20%.2

While tariff rates have increased markedly, government revenue from imports in the second quarter was only 0.2% of GDP, close to the 0.3% base case that we outlined in our 2025 Outlook.3 With only modest evidence of adverse impacts from tariffs, strong fundamentals for large-cap companies and the steady upward march in asset prices suggested that investors had moved on from the tariff risks. But that notion was seemingly shattered on October 10 when the White House hinted at the possibility of significant tariff increases on China and equity markets moved lower.4

The Treasury and Commerce Departments negotiating with the U.S.’ major trading partners have made progress, but thus far it’s a patchwork of agreements and announcements. So, in this piece, we aim to provide a one-stop tariff shop, looking at the aggregate impact, details about agreements with the U.S.’ top 10 trading partners, and potential implications across the economy, markets, and investment themes.

Don’t Let the Sun Go Down on the Economy

Tariffs can impact the economy in numerous ways. For starters, tariffs function as a tax that shifts money from the capital-efficient private sector to the less-efficient public sector, creating a drag on capital’s productivity. They also raise costs for businesses, potentially threatening profit margins and earnings. Where possible, businesses will eventually look to pass on those costs through higher prices, which can dampen consumer demand and spending. So far, corporate profitability and consumer demand remain reasonably robust, but there are signs that the labor market and possibly the broader economy are slowing as new tariff policies are implemented.5

The actual increase in government revenue from imports has stepped up meaningfully in recent months. In January 2025, tariffs accounted for $7.3 billion of government revenue. By August, that number was $27.7 billion, an almost fourfold increase.6 The approximate tariff rate increased from 2.2% to 9.7%. At the start of the year, tariffs were about 1.1% of government outlays. By July, they were 4.4%, which is a relatively big gain in a short period, though the net impact on the economy is pretty modest. The government raised $64.4 billion in Q2 2025 on GDP of $30.5 trillion, thereby accounting for only about 0.2% of GDP.7

To date, there is little evidence that tariffs have adversely affected consumers or businesses at scale. August U.S. retail sales grew 5.0% year-over-year (YoY), averaged 4.4% growth since April and above the average growth since 2000 of 4.2%.8 Second-quarter personal consumption grew 2.4% from the prior year and is also above the 25-year average of 2.2%.9 Corporate operating expenses have also remained reasonably subdued. The White House has seemingly figured out a way to increase taxes modestly without Congress having to vote on the matter.

Manufacturing and construction employment remain weak. Three-month rolling averages show that jobs in both sectors have been declining since the White House’s April announcement, which is interesting because tariffs are designed, in part, to protect domestic jobs.10 Labor weakness is probably the biggest risk to the economy at present, and the Federal Reserve has noted rising risk in employment.

You Gotta Tariff Someone

Below, we break down the complex tariff landscape for the U.S. and top 10 trading partners, which together account for approximately 80% of U.S. imports.11 Negotiations are at varying stages, with tariff rates ranging widely, from 10% to 58%, and various carveouts included in many of the deals.

European Union (EU): A framework is in place, but a final agreement remains uncertain. Many different interests are at play given the economic differences across member states. The current framework calls for reducing auto tariffs on U.S. imports from 27.5% to 15%, while the EU must eliminate tariffs on all U.S. industrial goods, including autos, which are currently subject to a 10% tariff.12 The U.S. has increasingly focused on rules that could adversely impact U.S. tech companies, and more recently the U.S. sought exemptions on climate and corporate compliance rules. The steel and aluminum 50% tariffs still apply, while pharmaceutical and semiconductor imports are capped at 15%.13

Mexico: The U.S. and Mexico are negotiating with the first six-year review of the U.S-Mexica-Canada trade deal (USMCA) coming up in 2026. The current tariff rate is 25%, but it is set to rise another 5% after the end of a 90-day pause expiring November 1.14 That said, all goods that comply with the current USMCA terms are duty free, which account for most of the imports. A new tariff set for November 1 applies to medium- and heavy-duty trucks, as import volumes have tripled since 2019.15 Section 232 tariffs, imposed in the name of national security under the Trade Expansion Act of 1962, are in effect at 50% for steel, copper, and aluminum.16 In the current scenario, more than 80% of goods might not be subject to the tariffs.

China: The negotiations between the U.S. and China are very delicate and the source of continued market angst. Prior to the current round of negotiations, there was a 25% tariff on approximately 65% of the imports from China.17 The U.S. has moved to close loopholes such as the de minimis rule so that tariffs apply to 100% of imports. Rates have also been raised, including an additional 10% reciprocal increase and another 20% tied to fentanyl, bringing the effective tariff rate to roughly 58% on Chinese imports versus 33% on U.S. exports.18 The current pause is set to expire on November 10. Adding complexity to these negotiations is that they also encompass intellectual property, technology transfer, market access, rare earths, and geopolitical allegiances.

Canada: The relationship between the U.S. and Canada has deteriorated since the start of the year, and negotiations seem stalled. Canada has generally adopted a tit-for-tat strategy in response to U.S. tariffs of 25%.19 Canada responded with tariffs on the non-North America parts of imported cars. Canada has taken a more measured approach to materials and natural resources, even after the 50% tariffs on steel and aluminum. The U.S. is expected to drop tariffs on USMCA-compliant goods.20

Japan: The U.S. and Japan reached a trade agreement that is reasonably straightforward. Rather than follow a sector-based template like in other negotiations, the U.S. will apply a 15% tariff on most goods, including autos, which were previously subject to a 25% rate.21 The agreement includes $550 billion of pledged capital investment in the U.S. through loans and guarantees. Japan also agreed to buy an additional $8 billion in agricultural products and increase rice imports by 75% above the existing quota.22 Reducing non-tariff barriers has generally helped improve U.S. export volumes. The agreement does not cover pharmaceuticals and semiconductors, which will likely be negotiated later.23

South Korea: The U.S. and South Korea hope to finalize a deal by the end of October. The current framework calls for a 15% tariff, below the 25% rate announced on April 2.24 It includes no tariffs on U.S. exports. Autos represent about a quarter of U.S. imports from South Korea. Sensitive product areas such as computer chips, batteries, machinery, and pharmaceuticals are currently excluded.25 Copper, steel, and aluminum will be tariffed at 50%, while South Korea will have carveouts on U.S. beef and rice exports. The framework also calls for $350 billion of South Korean investments in the U.S. through loan guarantees, including $150 billion for shipbuilding and $100 billion for liquid natural gas (LNG).26 Negotiations have stalled over these terms, with South Korea looking to hedge currency risk through a bilateral currency swap and the U.S. looking for cash and equity investment rather than loans.27

Taiwan: The U.S. initially proposed a 32% tariff rate, but it is currently at 20%, with negotiations ongoing and Taiwan aiming for a lower rate.28 At present, semiconductors and electric goods are exempt from the tariff, which largely applies to machinery and plastics. The U.S. also imports items such as screws, bolts, nuts, motor vehicle parts, electrical transformers, and power supplies that are subject to current tariff rates.29 U.S. imports from Taiwan have been growing much faster than exports, an imbalance that the U.S. wants to change by bringing 50% of semiconductor manufacturing onshore.30 Taiwan Semiconductor pledged a $165 investment in a U.S. facility, and there is a proposal for a $10 billion increase in U.S. agricultural exports over four years.31 The U.S., however, seeks a more comprehensive investment plan.

Vietnam: The U.S. currently has a 20% tariff on imports from Vietnam and a 40% rate on goods shipped through the country to deter manufacturers attempting to mask the origin.32 These rates are below the April 2 figure of 46%. Vietnam is opening its market to U.S. goods with no tariffs. On the other side, the U.S. is Vietnam’s largest export market, with computer parts, machinery, appliances, clothing, shoes, and other consumer goods the top products.33 The U.S.’ trade deficit with Vietnam is particularly high, running over $100 billion per year.34 Copper, aluminum, and steel will be tariffed at 50%.

United Kingdom (UK): The U.S. runs a trade surplus with the UK and exports about $80 billion in goods.35 A trade deal that sets the U.S. tariff rate at 10% is in place, but it has some interesting features. For example, UK auto exports will be charged the 10% rate up to 100,000 units, and then a 25% rate on any additional vehicles.36 UK steel is only subject to a 25% tariff, below the 50% for most countries, but copper imports are charged the higher rate. The deal also includes provisions for $700 million in U.S. ethanol exports and $250 million in agricultural products such as beef.37

India: The U.S. and India have struggled to reach an agreement, despite a historically good relationship between the leaders. The U.S. imposed 50% tariffs on Indian goods as of August 27, in part as a penalty for India purchasing Russian oil purchases.38 Previously, the tariff rate was 25%. The U.S. is India’s largest export market, approximately three times larger than its next trading partners. The higher tariff rate could meaningfully reduce U.S. imports from India and affect industries such as jewelry, textiles, footwear, carpets, and agricultural goods.39 Imports of electronics, pharma, energy, and critical mineral exports are currently exempt.40 India has typically resisted opening their market to U.S. agricultural goods, which has been a challenge in negotiations.41

Guess That’s Why Some Sectors Will (and Will Not) Call It The Blues

The tariff impact will not be uniform across sectors and industries. In the end, certain areas are likely to be harder hit, while others manage to operate relatively unscathed.

Two areas that seem relatively insulated from the tariff battle are services and high-tech products. The service sector has been notably excluded from tariff discussions, which have focused almost entirely on the cross-border movement of goods. The U.S. exports more than $1 trillion in services annually, making it the largest service exporter in the world.42 Operating expenses for service businesses could rise modestly from input price increases, but a major shift in demand at home or abroad is unlikely.

Carveouts for high-tech products such as semiconductors and memory chips suggest sensitivity around technology tied to the AI buildout and productivity enhancements. The U.S. has long relied on foreign imports of products like semiconductors, and the CHIPS Act of 2022—which provides public subsidies for onshoring tech manufacturing— underscores the challenges of redirecting both demand and production.43

Taken together, tariffs may be less of a concern for tech services companies. AI companies are some of the biggest beneficiaries of U.S. efforts to ensure supplies of necessary components and to potentially limit others’ access to premium technologies. Cybersecurity firms provide services to clients across the globe, and their biggest exposure to the trade conflict is ensuring sufficient access to compute capabilities. The story is similar for cloud computing companies.

Social media and fintech are interesting segments to examine because they provide services that are not subject to tariffs, but they may be sensitive to a slowdown in consumer activity should tariffs erode confidence in the labor market.

Domestic producers of copper, steel, and aluminum may be well positioned to benefit from Section 232 tariffs. The White House first announced 25% rates on steel and aluminum in February, subsequently increasing them to 50% in July. Then the administration announced 50% tariffs on copper imports in July.44 U.S. materials and commodity companies focused on the U.S. market, including areas such as infrastructure development, could see demand increase given the higher cost of international goods and realize some improved pricing power. Copper producers could benefit given the combination of data center buildouts and global demand from infrastructure.

Another industry potentially well positioned is U.S. natural gas and its support infrastructure, such as pipelines. The U.S. is the world’s largest exporter of natural gas, which may be a focus for negotiators aiming to reduce trade imbalances. Daily U.S. exports of natural gas increased from around 43,000 metric tons in 2017 to 297,000 in 2025, an almost 700% increase.45 While domestic demand remains strong amid rising electricity needs, growing foreign demand may be another source of revenue.

Some industries are likely to absorb a higher share of costs, and their ability to pass price increases to customers may vary. Retailers, for example, may well get pinched given the heavy reliance on imports in the consumer goods market. Recent reports show little evidence of significant price increases, potentially suggesting intense competition and slowing demand have limited retailers’ pricing power.46 Third-quarter sales and profit margins are likely to offer better insight now that tariffs have been in place for some time.

Another segment potentially facing headwinds in the near term are industrials that rely on physical inputs like natural resources. With 50% tariffs on copper, steel, and aluminum tariffs, costs for auto and heavy equipment manufacturers could rise meaningfully.47 Materials tied to items such as fasteners and electrical equipment could also weigh on profits. B2B industrials may be better positioned to pass higher costs on to customers than consumer-facing firms. That said, companies at the forefront of innovation in areas such as robotics and automation may have an easier time sustaining margins.
